title: Distributed Tracing with OpenTelemetry: From Instrumentation to Visualization
description: A practical checklist for adding OpenTelemetry tracing to your services, shipping spans through the Collector, and turning that data into something you can actually debug with.

items.title: Map your services and pick the first flows to trace
items.description: Don't try to instrument everything on day one. Pick two or three request flows that hurt the most when they break (checkout, login, async job pipeline) and trace those end to end. You learn the tools faster, and the data is useful immediately instead of being a wall of green spans nobody reads.

codeBlocks.label: Tracing rollout plan
codeBlocks.code:
# tracing-rollout.yaml
phase_1_flows:
  - name: checkout
    services: [web-bff, orders, payments, inventory, notifications]
    why: "Highest revenue impact, hardest to debug today"
    target_date: 2026-05-15

  - name: login
    services: [web-bff, auth, sessions, user-profile]
    why: "Mystery 2s p99, no idea where the time goes"
    target_date: 2026-05-22

phase_2_flows:
  - name: search
  - name: admin-reports

out_of_scope:
  - cron-jobs (low priority, batch)
  - internal-tooling (no users)

items.title: Deploy the OpenTelemetry Collector as your trace hub
items.description: Don't ship spans straight from apps to your backend. Run the Collector in front. It buffers data, lets you swap backends without touching app code, and is where you do sampling, redaction, and batching. Run it as a DaemonSet (agent) on each node and a Deployment (gateway) for cluster-wide processing.

codeBlocks.label: OTel Collector config (gateway)
codeBlocks.code:
# otel-collector-config.yaml
receivers:
  otlp:
    protocols:
      grpc:
        endpoint: 0.0.0.0:4317
      http:
        endpoint: 0.0.0.0:4318

processors:
  batch:
    timeout: 5s
    send_batch_size: 512
  memory_limiter:
    check_interval: 1s
    limit_mib: 1500
    spike_limit_mib: 512
  resource:
    attributes:
      - key: deployment.environment
        value: production
        action: insert

exporters:
  otlp/tempo:
    endpoint: tempo:4317
    tls:
      insecure: false

service:
  pipelines:
    traces:
      receivers: [otlp]
      processors: [memory_limiter, resource, batch]
      exporters: [otlp/tempo]

codeBlocks.label: Helm install
codeBlocks.code:
helm repo add open-telemetry https://open-telemetry.github.io/opentelemetry-helm-charts
helm repo update

helm upgrade --install otel-gateway open-telemetry/opentelemetry-collector \
  --namespace observability --create-namespace \
  --set mode=deployment \
  --set replicaCount=3 \
  --values otel-collector-config.yaml

items.title: Add auto-instrumentation to your applications
items.description: Auto-instrumentation gives you HTTP, gRPC, database, and message queue spans for free. Start there. You get 80% of the value with one import. Switch to manual spans only for the things auto-instrumentation can't see, like business logic.

codeBlocks.label: Node.js auto-instrumentation
Python zero-code instrumentation
codeBlocks.code:
# Install
pip install opentelemetry-distro opentelemetry-exporter-otlp
opentelemetry-bootstrap -a install

# Run your app -- no code changes needed
OTEL_SERVICE_NAME=orders-api \
OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT=http://otel-gateway:4317 \
OTEL_TRACES_EXPORTER=otlp \
opentelemetry-instrument python app.py

items.title: Propagate trace context across every service boundary
items.description: A trace that stops at the first hop is useless. Make sure W3C traceparent headers flow through every HTTP call, gRPC metadata, and message queue header. Auto-instrumentation handles this for HTTP and gRPC. For Kafka, RabbitMQ, or your own RPC, you wire it up by hand. Test it: pick a request, follow the traceparent header through every log line.

codeBlocks.label: Manual context propagation for Kafka
Verify traceparent header is forwarded
codeBlocks.code:
# Send a request with a known trace ID and check it appears downstream
curl -v http://api.example.com/checkout \
  -H 'traceparent: 00-0af7651916cd43dd8448eb211c80319c-b7ad6b7169203331-01'

# Then grep your downstream service logs for that trace ID
kubectl logs -n payments deploy/payments-api | grep 0af7651916cd43dd8448eb211c80319c

items.title: Pick a sampling strategy before your bill explodes
items.description: Tracing every request in production will cost you a fortune and drown the signal. Use head-based sampling (e.g. 10%) for high-volume services, and turn on tail-based sampling at the Collector to keep 100% of errors and slow requests. Always-sample errors. Never-sample health checks.

codeBlocks.label: Tail-based sampling at the Collector
codeBlocks.code:
processors:
  tail_sampling:
    decision_wait: 10s
    num_traces: 100000
    expected_new_traces_per_sec: 1000
    policies:
      - name: errors-policy
        type: status_code
        status_code: { status_codes: [ERROR] }

      - name: slow-requests-policy
        type: latency
        latency: { threshold_ms: 1000 }

      - name: health-checks-drop
        type: string_attribute
        string_attribute:
          key: http.target
          values: [/health, /ready, /metrics]
          invert_match: true

      - name: probabilistic-policy
        type: probabilistic
        probabilistic: { sampling_percentage: 10 }

service:
  pipelines:
    traces:
      receivers: [otlp]
      processors: [memory_limiter, tail_sampling, batch]
      exporters: [otlp/tempo]

codeBlocks.label: Head sampling via env var
codeBlocks.code:
# 10% sampling at the SDK -- cheaper than tail sampling but loses errors
OTEL_TRACES_SAMPLER=parentbased_traceidratio \
OTEL_TRACES_SAMPLER_ARG=0.1 \
node --require ./tracing.js app.js

items.title: Use semantic conventions for span attributes
items.description: Pick standard attribute names from the OpenTelemetry semantic conventions. If everyone uses http.request.method and db.system.name, your backend can build dashboards and alerts that work across services without per-team mapping. Inventing your own names like httpMethod or dbType breaks every UI feature that depends on standard fields.

codeBlocks.label: Custom span with semantic attributes

items.title: Never put secrets, tokens, or PII in span attributes
items.description: Spans get shipped to a backend, often a third-party SaaS, and indexed for search. Anything you put in an attribute is now searchable by anyone with backend access. Drop Authorization headers, credit card numbers, full email addresses, and request bodies at the Collector. Audit your traces before you trust the pipeline.

codeBlocks.label: Attribute redaction at the Collector
codeBlocks.code:
processors:
  attributes/redact:
    actions:
      - key: http.request.header.authorization
        action: delete
      - key: http.request.header.cookie
        action: delete
      - key: user.email
        action: hash
      - key: payment.card_number
        action: delete
      - key: db.statement
        pattern: '(password|token|secret)\s*=\s*\S+'
        action: update
        value: '[REDACTED]'

  redaction:
    allow_all_keys: true
    blocked_values:
      - '4[0-9]{12}(?:[0-9]{3})?'  # Visa
      - '[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Z|a-z]{2,}'  # email
    summary: silent

service:
  pipelines:
    traces:
      processors: [memory_limiter, attributes/redact, redaction, batch]

items.title: Correlate traces with logs and metrics
items.description: A trace ID in your logs turns 'something is slow' into 'this exact span on this exact request'. Inject the active trace ID into every log line. Add exemplars to metrics so you can click a latency spike on a graph and land on the trace that caused it. This is the part that pays for the whole investment.

codeBlocks.label: Inject trace context into logs
Grafana data source links (Tempo to Loki)
codeBlocks.code:
# tempo-datasource.yaml
apiVersion: 1
datasources:
  - name: Tempo
    type: tempo
    url: http://tempo:3200
    jsonData:
      tracesToLogsV2:
        datasourceUid: loki
        spanStartTimeShift: -1h
        spanEndTimeShift: 1h
        tags: ['service.name', 'pod']
        filterByTraceID: true
      tracesToMetrics:
        datasourceUid: prometheus
        queries:
          - name: 'Request rate'
            query: 'sum(rate(http_server_request_duration_seconds_count{$$__tags}[5m]))'
      serviceMap:
        datasourceUid: prometheus

items.title: Stand up a tracing backend and visualize your first traces
items.description: Pick a backend before you turn the firehose on. Self-hosted: Jaeger or Grafana Tempo (S3/GCS-backed, cheap at scale). SaaS: Honeycomb, Datadog, Lightstep. Tempo plus Grafana is the default open-source stack today. Don't pick on features alone, factor in storage cost and how your team already runs Grafana.

codeBlocks.label: Tempo with S3 backend
codeBlocks.code:
# tempo-values.yaml
tempo:
  storage:
    trace:
      backend: s3
      s3:
        bucket: company-tempo-traces
        endpoint: s3.amazonaws.com
        region: us-east-1
  retention: 720h  # 30 days
  receivers:
    otlp:
      protocols:
        grpc:
          endpoint: 0.0.0.0:4317
        http:
          endpoint: 0.0.0.0:4318
  metricsGenerator:
    enabled: true
    remoteWriteUrl: http://prometheus:9090/api/v1/write

codeBlocks.label: Query traces from the CLI
codeBlocks.code:
# Find recent traces for orders service with errors
curl -G http://tempo:3200/api/search \
  --data-urlencode 'tags=service.name=orders-api' \
  --data-urlencode 'tags=status=error' \
  --data-urlencode 'limit=20' \
  --data-urlencode 'start=2026-05-01T00:00:00Z' | jq .

# Fetch a specific trace by ID
curl http://tempo:3200/api/traces/0af7651916cd43dd8448eb211c80319c | jq .

items.title: Use traces to find latency hot spots, not just admire them
items.description: The point of tracing is to answer 'where did the time go'. Open a slow trace, find the longest span, ask why. Look for chatty database calls (N+1 queries are obvious in a flame graph), serial fan-out that should be parallel, and external API calls that don't have timeouts. If you can't articulate what you fixed last week using trace data, your team isn't actually using it.

codeBlocks.label: TraceQL for slow traces
codeBlocks.code:
# Slow checkout traces (over 2s)
{ resource.service.name="orders-api" && name="POST /checkout" } | duration > 2s

# Traces where the DB took more than half the total time
{ name="POST /checkout" } | by(trace:duration) | select(
  span.duration{db.system="postgresql"} / trace:duration > 0.5
)

# Find N+1 queries: same SQL repeated > 10 times in one trace
{ db.statement=~"SELECT.*FROM users.*" } | count_over_time() > 10

items.title: Monitor the Collector itself
items.description: If the Collector is dropping spans, you have observability gaps and won't know it. Scrape its metrics endpoint and alert on refused spans, queue saturation, and exporter failures. The Collector ships with Prometheus metrics out of the box.

codeBlocks.label: Alert on Collector dropping spans
codeBlocks.code:
groups:
  - name: otel-collector
    rules:
      - alert: OtelCollectorDroppingSpans
        expr: |
          rate(otelcol_exporter_send_failed_spans[5m]) > 0
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "OTel Collector failing to export spans"
          description: "Exporter {{ $labels.exporter }} is failing. Check backend connectivity."

      - alert: OtelCollectorQueueSaturated
        expr: |
          otelcol_exporter_queue_size / otelcol_exporter_queue_capacity > 0.8
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "OTel Collector export queue is filling up"

      - alert: OtelCollectorRefusingSpans
        expr: |
          rate(otelcol_receiver_refused_spans[5m]) > 0
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "Collector is refusing incoming spans -- memory limiter or rate limit hit"

items.title: Write a one-page tracing runbook for your team
items.description: If only one engineer knows how to read a flame graph, the investment dies the moment they go on vacation. Write a short page: how to find traces for an incident, how to read a span tree, what attributes you set, and where to add custom spans. Pin it in the on-call channel.

codeBlocks.label: Tracing runbook template
codeBlocks.code:
# Tracing Runbook

## Finding traces during an incident
1. Go to Grafana > Explore > Tempo
2. Search by `service.name` and time range, or paste a trace ID from a log line
3. Sort by duration to find the slowest requests

## Reading a flame graph
- Width = time spent in that span
- Children stacked under parent = work the parent waited on
- Gaps in the tree = unmeasured time (add a span)

## Adding a custom span
```ts
import { trace } from '@opentelemetry/api';
const tracer = trace.getTracer('my-service');
await tracer.startActiveSpan('my-operation', async (span) => {
  span.setAttribute('thing.id', id);
  try { await doWork(); } finally { span.end(); }
});
```

## Standard attributes we set everywhere
- `tenant.id`
- `user.id` (hashed at the collector)
- `feature.flag.<name>` for active flags

## Who to ping
- Pipeline issues: #observability
- Service-specific: service owner in CODEOWNERS
